sentences.base_language: My friend looks a bit sad today.
sentences.target_language: 我的朋友今天看起来有点难过。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ de péngyǒu jīntiān kàn qǐlái yǒudiǎn nánguò.
sentences.grammarNote: 看起来 (kàn qǐlái) = 'looks like / appears to'. This verb + 起来 pattern describes sensory impressions: 看起来 (looks), 听起来 (sounds), 闻起来 (smells), 吃起来 (tastes). 有点 before an adjective = 'a little / somewhat' (with a slightly negative tone).

sentences.romanized: Wǒ wèn tā fāshēng le shénme shì.
sentences.grammarNote: 发生了什么 = 'what happened'. 了 after the verb marks that the event occurred. Embedded questions in Chinese don't change word order: 我问他发生了什么 (I ask him what happened) — no inversion needed.

sentences.base_language: He says he had an argument with his girlfriend.
sentences.target_language: 他说他跟女朋友吵架了。
sentences.romanized: Tā shuō tā gēn nǚpéngyǒu chǎojià le.
sentences.grammarNote: 跟 (gēn) = 'with (a person)'. Used for doing something together with or against someone: 跟朋友聊天 (chat with a friend), 跟他吵架 (argue with him). 吵架了 = 'had an argument' (the 了 marks it as completed).
听到 = 'hear (and receive the info)' — 到 is a resultative complement showing the action succeeded. Compare: 听 (trying to hear) vs 听到 (actually heard it). 遗憾 = regret/sorry to hear.

sentences.base_language: I tell him it will be okay.
sentences.target_language: 我告诉他一切都会好的。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ gàosù tā yīqiè dōu huì hǎo de.
sentences.grammarNote: 一切 (yīqiè) = 'everything / all'. When used with 都, it emphasizes totality: 一切都会好 = 'everything will be okay'. 会…的 is a confident prediction pattern.
互相 (hùxiāng) = 'mutually / each other'. It comes before the verb: 互相帮助 (help each other), 互相理解 (understand each other). Chinese uses this adverb where English uses 'each other'.

sentences.base_language: He feels a little better after talking.
sentences.target_language: 聊完后他感觉好一些了。
sentences.romanized: Liáo wán hòu tā gǎnjué hǎo yīxiē le.
sentences.grammarNote: 好一些 = 'a bit better'. 一些 (yīxiē) after an adjective = 'a bit / somewhat': 好一些 (a bit better), 快一些 (a bit faster). The sentence-final 了 marks the change: he has now become somewhat better.
